title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Spin Doctor: The Yo-Yo Master
content.text: In his youth, Lawrence Sayegh dreamt of performing in vaudeville, but his career took a different spin. He became nine-time world champion and international master trickster of the yo-yo. Today, at 64, he is still spinning, much to everyone's delight. (37) [37] Sayegh says he was searching for a sport when he found the yo-yo. [/37] 'I was too short for basketball, too light for football, too nervous for baseball,' he says. When the Duncan Yo-Yo Company began holding neighbourhood competitions in the 1940s, he won every contest within biking distance of his home. || His parents, however, were sceptical of their son's talent. They had a mom-and-pop grocery store and were very much of the old school: 'work, work, work; make something of yourself,' he says. (38) [38] But after he graduated from high school, the president of Duncan Yo-Yo contacted Sayegh and offered him a job. [/38]  (39) [39] Duncan sent its new recruit off to demonstrate the company's product in the South and Southwest of the United States, and in Europe. [/39] || In 1952, the company showed Sayegh off at a press conference in Paris. At the time, Sayegh says, the yo-yo was even more popular in Europe than in the US. (40) [40] He was a huge hit. [/40] Actualités, the French newsreel, filmed Sayegh and his yo-yo tricks in settings all around Paris. || When Duncan Yo-Yo went out of business, Sayegh began manufacturing yo-yos of his own patented design. (41) [41] He runs the shop alone and has produced more than a million yo-yos, all of which he has sold through private shows. [/41] || These days, the yo-yo is enjoying a resurgence in popularity. (42) [42] 'Fifty per cent in my shop and fifty per cent doing shows. This is nice, quiet solitude,' he says, surveying his factory domain. [/42]

paragraphs.A: His assembly line, a mesmerising contraption of bicycle parts, allows him to work alone.
paragraphs.B: But from the beginning, he seemed to have a special knack for the spinning toy on a string.
paragraphs.C: Sayegh finds himself living in what he calls the best of both worlds.
paragraphs.D: The family agreed he should take it, realizing it was a real career.
paragraphs.E: There, he demonstrated tricks of his own devising, with such names as 'Pistol Pete'.
paragraphs.F: He may also be the only person in the world who can send two yo-yos flying off while doing the limbo.
paragraphs.G: It wasn't always obvious he had this skill to those closest to him.

questions.explanation: B introduces his natural talent before discussing his failures in other sports.
D shows the family's change of attitude towards the formal job offer.
F adds a physical feat just before describing his international tours.
E details what exactly he was doing in Paris to be considered a success.
A explains how his personal factory works in a solitary way.
C introduces the final reflection on his current life balance.
